entry.title: [BIP-0054] 64-Byte Transactions and Potential Legitimate Uses
entry.summary:
The discussion on BIP-0054's specification concerning the 64-byte transaction size reveals some important nuances and potential misunderstandings within the Bitcoin development community. BIP-0054 specifically invalidates transactions whose witness-stripped serialized size is exactly 64 bytes. This definition aims to clarify that only those transactions with exactly 64 bytes of non-witness data fall under this restriction, a detail that might be overlooked leading to confusion about what constitutes an invalid transaction under this proposal.
There are practical implications and current uses of 64-byte transactions that contradict the belief stated in BIP-0054 that such transactions are essentially useless. For instance, these transactions can be used to donate to future miners from a segwit output or as a connector output for claims by a miner at a later time. Additionally, they can serve as ephemeral anchor outputs, possibly after a certain delay, to add a subsidy ability to transactions.
Looking forward, there are anticipated developments that could leverage 64-byte transactions in innovative ways. These include using output scripts for transaction sponsor encodings, encoding time-of-creation run scripts through future covenants work, and potentially creating more complex state machines with introspection opcodes. Such advancements suggest that while currently the utility of 64-byte transactions is recognized, future applications may prove even more significant.
Given these considerations, it has been proposed to revise the language in BIP-0054. The revision would acknowledge the documented use cases that make the invalidation of 64-byte transactions problematic and reflect a more nuanced view of their potential value. This adjustment would provide a clearer and more accurate perspective on the implications of the BIP-0054 ruling within the developer community and for the evolution of Bitcoin's technical foundations.
